content.problemStatement:
Chef and Chefina are playing the famous game of Rock-Paper-Scissors.

The game consists of NNN rounds. In each round, both players choose one of the three moves: Rock, Paper, or Scissors, denoted by `R`, `P`, and `S` respectively.


If both players play the same move, the current round ends in a draw and neither of them gets a point.
If one of the players plays Rock and the other plays Scissors, the player who played Rock wins the round and gets a point.
If one of the players plays Scissors and the other plays Paper, the player who played Scissors wins the round and gets a point.
If one of the players plays Paper and the other plays Rock, the player who played Paper wins the round and gets a point.
You are given a string AAA of length NNN denoting the moves made by Chefina in NNN rounds.

To win the game, Chef needs to score **more than** ⌊N2⌋\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \rfloor⌊2N​⌋ points.

Find the **lexicographically smallest** sequence of moves that wins the game for Chef.

Note:


⌊x⌋\lfloor x \rfloor⌊x⌋ denotes the floor function. For example, ⌊32⌋=1.\lfloor \frac{3}{2} \rfloor = 1.⌊23​⌋=1.
A string XXX is said to be lexicographically smaller than string YYY, if, for the first position where XXX and YYY differ, Xi<YiX_i \lt Y_iXi​<Yi​.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integers NNN — the length of the string.
The next line contains a string AAA of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **lexicographically smallest** string of length NNN which wins the game for Chef.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the string. The second line contains a string A of length N consisting only of characters 'R', 'P' and 'S' (Chefina's moves).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the lexicographically smallest string of length N that makes Chef win strictly more than ⌊N/2⌋ rounds.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Chef needs 1 win, the only winning move against 'R' is 'P'.\nTest case 2: N=2, need 2 wins. Winning moves are 'S' against 'P' and 'R' against 'S', so the only winning string is "SR".\nTest case 3: N=3, need 2 wins. Possible winning strings are many; the lexicographically smallest among them is "PPS".

solution.approach: For each round i we know which move beats Chefina's move: win[i] = 'P' if A[i]=='R', 'S' if A[i]=='P', 'R' if A[i]=='S'. Chef needs at least need = floor(N/2)+1 winning rounds. We construct the answer from left to right. At position i we try the three possible characters in lexicographic order ('P','R','S'). If we choose character c, the remaining number of wins required becomes need' = need - (c == win[i] ? 1 : 0). The construction is feasible iff need' ≤ remaining positions (N-i-1) and need' ≥ 0. The first character that satisfies the condition is fixed for this position. This greedy choice is optimal because we always keep the smallest possible prefix while preserving the ability to reach the required number of wins later. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total N) where total N is the sum of lengths over all test cases (≤ 2·10^6).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the output string of each test case (output itself).
